quality_notes: Remove unsupported numeric claims and metrics unless the topic explicitly provided them.
Remove unsupported hard stats (percentages, millions, salary figures) unless provided in the topic.
Add more relevant emojis to improve scanability and engagement (target: 4 to 10).
Align the post more closely to the planned diagram labels so the image and text tell the same story.

post_text:
📌 FinOps for AI Systems

I'm calling it - the AI-native cloud infrastructure space just got a whole lot more interesting with Railway securing $100 million in funding. As a Staff Engineer, I've seen many claim that AWS is the only viable option for cloud infrastructure, but I strongly disagree. The idea that you need to break the bank to have a reliable and scalable cloud setup is outdated.

Newsflash: Railway has already amassed two million developers without spending a dime on marketing, and their AI-native approach is a major reason why. I believe Railway's focus on automation and machine learning will give them a significant edge over AWS in the long run.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Traditional Cloud] -->|manual config|> B[Human Error]
    C[AI-Native Cloud] -->|automated config|> D[Scalability]
    D -->|lower costs|> E[Increased Adoption]
```

Railway's AI-native approach means that many of the tedious and error-prone tasks associated with traditional cloud infrastructure are automated, freeing up developers to focus on what really matters - writing code. With two million developers already on board, I think it's clear that Railway is onto something big. The question is, will AWS be able to keep up with the likes of Railway, or will they become the next relic of the cloud infrastructure space? 💬 Can AWS really compete with the agility and innovation of a company like Railway? #AInativeCloud #CloudInfrastructure #Railway #AWS #CloudComputing #DevOps
